summary: I was born in Kazakhstan and lived there for the first three years of my life before moving to Syria. Although I don't have many memories from Kazakhstan itself, it is the place where my life began. My early childhood there set the stage for my upbringing in Syria.

I was raised in Syria after moving there from Kazakhstan when I was very young. Growing up in Syria was full of memories with my family and sisters, and it shaped who I am today. However, living there during the Syrian civil war was very difficult, especially since we lived near the Russian Embassy where bombings occurred. My experiences in Syria, including the violence and community divisions, deeply impacted me and my family.

I first heard about the Syrian civil war when I was eleven years old, watching the news with my family. Initially, we thought it would be a short conflict, but it quickly escalated and moved closer to where we lived. The war brought violence right outside my window, including bombings near our home by the Russian Embassy. The conflict forced my family to eventually resettle because of the danger and political persecution we faced.

Our home was located right next to the Russian Embassy in Syria, which became a very dangerous place during the civil war. There were bombings exchanged between the embassy and others, and one bomb even hit a floor beneath us by mistake. Living so close to the embassy meant constant fear and danger for me and my family during that time.

My older sister had to move to Dubai by herself because she couldn't come with us to the United States due to immigration complications. She has been living there independently, and unfortunately, our youngest sister has never met her because of the distance and visa issues. We even missed her wedding two months ago and had to watch it over video.

My family and I resettled in the United States after fleeing the dangers of the Syrian civil war. The process was difficult, especially because my older sister could not come with us and had to stay in Dubai. Living in the US has been a chance to build a new life, though the separation from family and challenges like visa denials have been hard.

The COVID-19 pandemic added another layer of difficulty to our lives, especially regarding travel and family reunification. For example, my youngest sister was unable to get a visa to visit the US for my high school graduation because of the pandemic and refugee status concerns. The pandemic has made it harder to maintain connections with family spread across different countries.

I was raised as a Muslim, and my faith has been an important part of how I coped with the hardships of the civil war and displacement. Although there are moments when I doubted God, especially during traumatic times, my belief in Islam helped me find hope and strength. Faith gave me comfort and a sense of community amid chaos.
